coredata.dc:title: Facile microwave synthesis of Zinc Ferrite@NCDs for photocatalytic degradation of fluoroquinolone antibiotics
coredata.dc:creator: Kaushal, Neha
Sarraf, Sonu
Basu, Aviru Kumar
Mishra, Sunita
Saha, Avishek
coredata.dc:description: Zinc ferrite (ZnFe2O4) composites have attracted growing interest in photocatalytic water purification. Herein, the hybrids of zinc ferrite and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (ZnF@x%NCDs) were synthesized via facile microwave synthesis. X-ray diffraction and Raman characterization confirm the formation of the spinel structure, and elemental composition has been analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The photocatalytic activity of ZnF@x%NCDs was evaluated via the decomposition of ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin under visible light illumination. We noted that 20 % weight loading of NCDs led to the highest (∼79.2 %) ciprofloxacin (CIP) degradation. Diffuse reflectance measurements demonstrate the reduction of the band gap in composites ZnF@20%NCDs (1.91 eV) compared with bare ZnF (2.03 eV); moreover, the hybridization of zinc ferrites with NCDs results in enhanced photocurrent density and surface area. The effect of experimental parameters, including the role of photocatalyst dosage, pH value, and initial concentration of pollutants on the degradation of CIP, have also been investigated.

head.ce:title: Facile microwave synthesis of Zinc Ferrite@NCDs for photocatalytic degradation of fluoroquinolone antibiotics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc ferrite (ZnFe2O4) composites have attracted growing interest in photocatalytic water purification. Herein, the hybrids of zinc ferrite and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (ZnF@x%NCDs) were synthesized via facile microwave synthesis. X-ray diffraction and Raman characterization confirm the formation of the spinel structure, and elemental composition has been analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The photocatalytic activity of ZnF@x%NCDs was evaluated via the decomposition of ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin under visible light illumination. We noted that 20 % weight loading of NCDs led to the highest (∼79.2 %) ciprofloxacin (CIP) degradation. Diffuse reflectance measurements demonstrate the reduction of the band gap in composites ZnF@20%NCDs (1.91 eV) compared with bare ZnF (2.03 eV); moreover, the hybridization of zinc ferrites with NCDs results in enhanced photocurrent density and surface area. The effect of experimental parameters, including the role of photocatalyst dosage, pH value, and initial concentration of pollutants on the degradation of CIP, have also been investigated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Removal of widespread environmental pollutants is one of the significant challenges in the current decade. Antibiotic pharmaceuticals have emerged as the leading cause of water pollution among various emerging contaminants. For example, an upsurge in the use and discharge of ciprofloxacin deteriorates the aquatic system, resulting in environmental pollution [1,2]. In this context, heterogeneous photocatalysis has emerged as a potential candidate for cost-effective water purification [3,4]. However, the practical applications of photocatalytic water remediation involve several challenges. Conventional metal-oxide based photocatalysts, such as TiO2, ZnO, etc., are not active in the visible light of the solar light radiation [5–8]. Recently, ferrites have been studied extensively for photocatalytic water treatment [9–11]. Their properties, such as better chemical stability, small bandgaps, and low cost, attracted environmental researchers to employ heterogeneous photocatalysis. Usually, ferrites demonstrate spinel structure with the oxide ions occupying face-centered cubic lattice. The (M) metal and (Fe) iron cations are distributed in the tetrahedral and the octahedral sites, respectively. Among these ferrites, spinel zinc ferrite has been widely investigated for photocatalytic water treatment due to its narrower band gap (1.9 eV), chemical stability, promising magnetic properties, etc. [12]. However, bare zinc ferrites have moderate photocatalytic performance due to inefficient charge separation, inefficient hydroxyl radical production, and faster recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs [9,13,14].

Different modification techniques have been explored to improve the photocatalytic performance of zinc ferrite by metal doping [12,15–17], hybridization with noble metal nanoparticles [18,19], semiconductors [20–24], etc. However, in most of these studies, the excessive use of toxic and expensive metals and tedious synthesis protocol limits their potential application. In addition, most of the reported photocatalytic activity of zinc ferrite is usually performed with organic dyes as model pollutants, including methylene blue [15,25,26], methyl orange [27,28], rhodamine B [15,24], etc. In this regard, combining zinc ferrite with inexpensive, environmentally friendly, photoactive carbonaceous materials could offer a better strategy for more efficient photocatalytic performance. Among the carbonaceous materials, (CDs) carbon dots/(CQDs) carbon quantum dots have emerged as promising light-harvesting nanomaterials because of their excellent light absorption and unique electron donor-accepting properties [29,30].

Photo-induced charge transfer properties of CDs can be further tuned by doping with heteroatoms [31]. In this context, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) may improve the photo-induced electron-hole separation at the interface of semiconductor photocatalysts, enhancing the overall photocatalytic performance [32]. Researchers have explored the role of NCDs in heterogeneous composites for improving photocatalytic efficiency. For instance, J. Zhang et al. have reported the NCDs mediated silver phosphate (Ag3PO4)/bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) based Z-scheme photocatalyst for tetracycline degradation [33]. In another report, R. Jiang et al. studied the CQDs/SnNb2O6/BiOCl system for the degradation of benzocaine, in which the CQDs acted as a bridge for the charge carriers [34]. However, it involves the usage of relatively toxic metal oxides, making the photocatalytic process less environmentally friendly. To this end, only a few reports have explored the Zinc Ferrite/Nitrogen-doped carbon dot composite for photocatalytic applications. Huang et al. have suggested the Zinc ferrite/CQDs composite for the NO removal. However, it involves using a multi-step and tedious hydrothermal approach to synthesize the final photocatalysts [32].

In this work, zinc ferrites were synthesized via a facile microwave-assisted method and employed to study the visible light-induced photocatalytic activity. Microwave synthesis has several advantages, including rapidity, better reproducibility, and efficiency compared to the conventional hydrothermal and sol-gel methods. Zinc ferrite composites with NCDs (ZnF@x%NCDs) have been fabricated to enhance visible light photocatalytic activity by varying the quantity of NCDs. Among these, ZnF@20%NCDs composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity for photo-transformation of ciprofloxacin in water. Moreover, we have examined the effect of different parameters, for instance, the role of the pH in an aqueous medium, the initial concentration of CIP, and the amount of photocatalyst on photocatalytic performance. We have also performed photocatalytic degradation of norfloxacin with ZnF@20%NCDs. To our knowledge, the photocatalytic activity of ZnF@NCDs composites for the degradation of fluoroquinolone antibiotics has not been reported.

Ciprofloxacin (≥98 %), Iron(III) acetylacetonate (97 %), Nafion 117 (∼5 %), sodium hydroxide (anhydrous, >98 %), and tetramethyl ammonium hydroxide pentahydrate (extrapure, 98 %) were obtained from Sigma. Zinc acetate dihydrate (99.5 %) and isopropyl alcohol (99.5 %) were obtained from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL-India). Ethanol (99.8 %) was purchased from Honeywell. Citric acid (>98 %), urea (>99 %), and p-Benzoquinone (>97 %) were purchased from TCI Chemicals. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, 99 %) was obtained from Loba Chemie. Norfloxacin was obtained from Norlet-400 tablets. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, ∼35 %) was purchased from Himedia Labs.

Solvent-free microwave synthesis of graphitic NCDs was done using citric acid (CA) and urea (U) as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively, with the CA: U weight ratio of 1:3 based on our previous reports [31]. The precursors were heated at 250 °C for 30 min in a microwave synthesis reactor (Monowave 200, Anton-Paar). After the reaction, the solid black product was extracted and grounded in a mortar pestle to get uniform powder. Then, purification was performed based on the previously modified method reported [35]. Briefly, the aqueous dispersion of the obtained powder was heated at 90 °C along with stirring for 60 min. The supernatant was removed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min at 25 °C, and the residual black powder was dried at 40 °C under vacuum for 24 h. The synthesized carbon dots are denoted as NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Zinc Ferrite and Zinc Ferrite/Nitrogen-doped Carbon Dots composites

ce:section.ce:para: A modified method was adopted to synthesize zinc ferrite [36]. Briefly, 0.9 mmol of zinc acetate dihydrate and 1.0 mmol of iron (III) acetylacetonate were dissolved in 5 mL of ethanol. In another beaker, 5 mL solution of tetramethylammonium hydroxide pentahydrate (2.0 mmol) in ethanol was prepared. Finally, these two solutions were mixed and sonicated for the next 20 min till dark red color was obtained. Tetramethylammonium hydroxide pentahydrate acts as a mineralizer and capping agent [36]. The solution was irradiated in a microwave synthesis reactor at 180 °C for 30 min. The brown product was separated by centrifugation and washing with ethanol several times.
Finally, the obtained sample was dried under vacuum at 60 °C followed by calcination at 400 °C for 1 h. The as-synthesized zinc ferrite (ZnFe2O4) is designated as ZnF later in the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Zinc Ferrite/Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (ZnF@x%NCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A similar method was used for the synthesis of ZnF@x%NCDs composite nanomaterials. The different weight percentage of NCDs (x = 10, 20, 30) was added to the solution of zinc acetate dehydrate (0.9 mmol), iron (III) acetylacetonate (1.0 mmol), and tetramethylammonium hydroxide pentahydrate (2.0 mmol) in ethanol and exposed to microwave radiation with the same reaction conditions. The composites were separated by centrifugation, followed by washing and drying. Finally, the product was calcined at 400 °C and was denoted as ZnF@10%NCDs, ZnF@20%NCDs, and ZnF@30%NCDs. The typical process of synthesis is illustrated in (Fig. 1 ).

Photocatalysis experiments were done with an in-house light source, including a 250 W halogen lamp (Philips). The lamp irradiance (∼230 mW/cm2) was measured using Newport Power Meter (Model-843-R). The transmittance spectra of the halogen lamp light source were measured with the Stellarnet Bluewave Spectrometer, as shown in (Fig. S1). Ambient temperature during photocatalysis was maintained throughout the experiment by placing the reaction beaker inside the water container. Initially, 15 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in the 25 mL aqueous solution of 0.01 mg/mL CIP and stirred continuously under dark conditions for 30 min. After illumination, 1 mL aliquot of the reaction solution was extracted at fixed time intervals, followed by centrifugation and filtration with (a 0.2
μ
m Nylon syringe filter). The obtained samples were analyzed by UV–Vis Spectrophotometer at λ
max = 272 nm. The photocatalytic efficiency was measured using the equation:
eq. (1)

Here, C0 refers to the concentration of CIP solution at time t = 0, and Ct is the CIP concentration at a particular instance of time t. The amount of CIP was obtained from the calibration curve (Fig. S2).

To investigate the effect of pH on the photocatalytic process, the pH of the photocatalytic medium was modulated at pH 4, 7, 9, and 11 using 0.1 M HCl and NaOH solutions, along with 0.6 mg/mL photocatalyst and 10 mg/L CIP. To understand the effect of catalyst dosage on photocatalytic performance, the amount of photocatalyst was varied as 5, 15, 30, and 45 mg at neutral pH conditions, keeping the CIP concentration constant (10 mg/L). Also, the concentration of CIP was varied to 10, 20, and 40 mg/L to understand the effect of pollutant concentration on photocatalytic performance, keeping the photocatalyst dosage constant (0.6 mg/mL). For the photolysis process, bare CIP solution (10 mg/L, pH 7) was illuminated, and UV–Vis spectra were taken. Adsorption of CIP on the surface of the photocatalyst was investigated by stirring CIP solution (10 mg/L, pH 7) in the presence of ZnF@20%NCDs (0.6 mg/mL) under dark conditions.

Powder X-Ray Diffraction (P-XRD) of the sample was obtained with Model D8 Advance, Bruker AXS X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. The JCPDS matching was performed using X'pert High Score software. The Raman spectra were measured with a 514 nm laser diode, using the RENISHAW inVia Raman microscope system. The morphological analysis was investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with JEOL JSM 7610FPlus microscope. Further, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were recorded with a JEM-2100 (JEOL Ltd.) electron microscope. ImageJ software was employed for measuring the size distribution and the d-spacing values. The BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) surface area analysis was done using AutosorbiQ 2TRX (Quantachrome Instruments). X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were acquired using a ThermoFisher Scientific K-Alpha + XPS spectrometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrophotometer. The UV/Vis absorption/diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained using a Varian Cary 4000 UV–Vis double-beam spectrophotometer. The electrochemical analysis was done using the Metrohm Autolab system (Model No. PGSTAT302 N) with Nova 2.1 software. The zeta potential measurements were performed using Zetasizer NanoZS instruments (Malvern).

XRD patterns of pristine NCDs, pure ZnFe2O4, and ZnF@20%NCDs composite are depicted in (Fig. 2 ). The solid phase microwave synthesis of NCDs resulted in producing the crystalline phase of NCDs. The characteristic broad peak centered at 27° (2θ) indicates the (002) reflections appearing due to interlayer stacking of crystalline planes [28]. In addition, pure-phase, calcined ZnFe2O4 demonstrates a cubic spinel structure. The diffraction peaks at 2θ of 29.6°, 35.0°, 42.5°, 53.0°, 56.3°, and 62.0° can be assigned to the reflection of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) planes, respectively [37]. The peaks obtained are in accordance with the JCPDS file No. 00-022-1012, reported in previous literature as well [38–40]. The distinct diffraction peaks are observed for all ZnF@20%NCDs photocatalysts as in pure ZnFe2O4. However, the characteristic (002) planes of NCDs are not observed in the ZnF@x%NCDs photocatalysts; this might be due to the low weight percentage and high dispersity of NCDs in the composite [32]. The XRD pattern observed for ZnF@x%NCDs (here, x = 10 and 30) can be seen in supporting information (Fig. S3). We also note that the diffraction peak of all the ZnF@x%NCDs photocatalysts have minor shifts, indicating that the presence of NCDs does not alter the phase of ZnFe2O4 in the composites.

Raman Spectroscopy is a helpful tool to determine the vibrational modes and crystalline phase of nanomaterials. Fig. S4 presents the Raman spectra of NCDs, ZnF, and ZnF@20%NCDs from 200 to 2000 cm−1. The absence of typical bands of α-Fe2O3 (220, 227, 288, 292 cm−1) and γ- Fe2O3 (381, 718 cm−1) illustrates the formation of spinel ferrites without any impurities [14]. Usually, these structures correspond to the Fd-3m space group, typically consisting of a total of five Raman-active vibrational modes, including A1g existing between 600 and 800 cm−1, F2g modes (F2g (3) between 410 and 550 cm−1 and F2g (2) exists in the range 260–380 cm−1) and F2g (1) along with Eg vibrational mode occurs in the lower frequency range [21]. The three F2g modes are labeled as F2g (n); here, n = 3, 2, 1 representing the third, second, and first F2g modes.

In the case of ZnF, A1g mode appears at ∼667 cm−1, representing the symmetric stretching of oxygen in tetrahedral units (AO4) of the spinel structure [21]. The bands at ∼466 cm−1 (F2g (3)) and ∼329 cm−1 (F2g(2)) correspond to the symmetric and non-symmetric bending of oxygen atoms in metal-oxygen bonds in the BO6 octahedral units [14,38]. Also, the low-frequency (E2g and F2g (1)) modes exist in the ZnF sample, signifying the symmetric bending vibrations due to oxygen atoms in the octahedral sites and the translational motion of whole tetrahedral units [41]. Lower-intensity second-order vibrations can also be seen between 1000 and 1800 cm−1 [21].

Two distinct peaks in the Raman spectra of the NCDs were observed at ∼1362 and 1567 cm−1, which correspond to the D and G bands, respectively. The D band signifies the defects due to sp3 carbon atoms, and the G-band corresponds to the sp2 carbon atoms in the graphitic structure [40]. The ID/IG ratio for NCDs is 1, which confirms the graphitic character of NCDs [42].

In ZnF@20%NCDs, characteristic Raman peaks of spinel ferrite are present with minor variations compared to the bare ZnF. Also, the intensity of second-order vibrations is higher than the ZnF sample. However, no characteristic graphitic bands are observed in ZnF@20%NCDs; this might be due to the low concentration of NCDs in the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-Ray (EDX) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the as-prepared photocatalysts was examined by scanning electron microscope. As reported earlier in solvent-free microwave synthesis, the SEM image of NCDs (Fig. S5(a)) shows characteristic flake-like agglomerated nanoparticles [43,44]. Similarly, the SEM image of pure ZnF (Fig. S5(b)) displays agglomerated nanoparticles.

Similar cluster-like structures stacked on the surface of NCDs sheets can be observed in ZnF@20%NCDs composite (Fig. 3 (a)). This aggregation of nanoparticles might be due to the magnetic character of ferrites nanoparticles and the development of smaller crystallite structures, as evident from the P-XRD analysis [45]. The corresponding EDX analysis discloses that the ZnF@20%NCDs sample (Fig. S6) consists of Zn, Fe, O, C, and N elements, with corresponding atomic percentages as 1.9 %, 1.9 %, 23.9 %, 69.3 %, and 3.0 % respectively. Similarly, the EDX results obtained for NCDs and pure ZnF have been represented in (Fig. S7).

The information on the crystal planes and morphology of the photocatalyst was obtained by acquiring the TEM and HR-TEM images. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 3(b)) identifies the (220) and (311) miller planes corresponding to the 0.29 and 0.25 nm d-spacing value, which is consistent with spinel structure [23,40]. Also, the planes corresponding to the NCDs (002) for 0.32 nm d-spacing value were observed [32,46]. It could be concluded from the HR-TEM images that the ZnF@20%NCDs exhibit good crystallinity, as a clear and sharp fringe pattern has been observed. The mean particle size of ZnF@20%NCDs was calculated from the TEM image (Fig. 3(c)) as 4.4 nm with a standard deviation of 0.9 from the size distribution curve, represented in (Fig. S8(a)). Similarly, for ZnF and NCDs, particles of size 4.9 and 5.3 nm were observed, as shown in (Figs. S8(b and c)) using TEM images presented in (Figs. S9(a and c)), respectively. Also, the HR-TEM images demonstrate (Figs. S9(b and d)) the presence of characteristic high-intensity facets in ZnF and NCDs, which agrees with the previous literature reports [32].

The specific surface area can be correlated with the performance of the photocatalytic nanomaterials. Usually, the more the surface area, the more the photoactivity of nanomaterials. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm of all the samples follows the Type IV adsorption (Fig. S10) [20]. The hysteresis loop depicts the mesoporous nature of the ZnF and its composites with NCDs [47]. The ZnF@20%NCDs photocatalyst shows the highest BET (Brunauer–Emmett– Teller) surface area of 160 m2g-1, which accounts for its highest photoactivity due to the availability of more active sites. The surface area of ZnF and NCDs was obtained as 132.8 and 2.7 m2g-1, respectively, which is relatively low compared to ZnF@20%NCDs. Although the surface area of bare NCDs is low, it probably introduces more nanopores upon introduction in ZnF. The trend in the surface area of as-prepared photocatalysts likely to compliment the trend in their corresponding photoactivity as follows.
ZnF@20%NCDs > ZnF@10%NCDs > ZnF> ZnF@30%NCDs

The Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution (Fig. S11) shows a sharp peak for all the samples centered between 3 and 5 nm. The mean pore diameter, surface area, and pore volume of all the samples are represented in Table S1. Overall, the trend in the surface area can provide a prediction of photocatalytic performance, considering the relationship between surface area and photocatalytic activity.

The elemental composition and the corresponding metallic state identification for ZnF@20%NCDs have been explored by XPS analysis. The survey XPS spectra reveal the presence of Zn, Fe, O, C, and N elements in the as-synthesized ZnF@20%NCDs composite (Fig. 4 (a)). The deconvoluted (via Origin software) XPS spectra of Zn (2p), Fe (2p), C (1s), N(1s), and O (1s) of the analyzed sample were depicted in (Fig. 4(a–f)) respectively. The existence of divalent Zn2+ species at the tetrahedral sites has been confirmed by the presence of two peaks at 1021.5 and 1044.6 eV corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively, as shown in (Fig. 4(b)) [48,49]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Fe(2p) (Fig. 4(c)) consist of two prominent peaks at 711.08 and 725.2 eV, which can be assigned to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively [21,45]. Two more peaks at 718.7 and 732.9 eV are observed as characteristic satellite peaks. Moreover, Fe 2p3/2 signal further consists of two deconvoluted spin states centered at 712.7 and 711.08 eV, indicating the presence of Fe3+ at the octahedral sites [45]. These results show that octahedral and tetrahedral sites are occupied by Fe3+ and Zn2+ ions, respectively, confirming the formation of a typical spinel structure.

Four peaks (529.7, 530.8, 531.6, and 532.7 eV) can be identified in the deconvoluted O (1s) core level spectra (Fig. 4(f)), indicating the presence of different oxygen moieties. The 529.7 and 531.6 eV peaks correspond to the oxygen from the crystal lattice and the surface hydroxyl groups, respectively [32,45]. On the other hand, the peaks at 530.8 and 532.7 eV indicate the existence of Fe–O and Fe–C–O bonds, respectively [45]. These results imply good interactions and bonding arrangements exist between Fe and C groups in the ZnF@20%NCDs composite.

The appearance of C (1s) and N (1s) in the XPS spectrum of ZnF@20%NCDs in Fig. 4 (d) and (e), respectively, indicate the presence of NCDs. Furthermore, from the high-resolution spectrum of C (1s), three peaks can be fitted, which correspond to C–C (sp3-hybridized) at 284.6 eV, C–N at 285.7 eV, and CO/CN at 288.7 eV, corroborating the presence of NCDs [50–52]. The N (1s) spectra are deconvoluted into three broad peaks at 398.2, 399.2, and 400.5 eV, corresponding to the presence of pyridinic, pyrrolic, and tertiary nitrogen moieties, respectively [31,50,52]. Furthermore, the XPS spectra for pristine NCDs and ZnF are shown in the supplementary information (Fig. S12) and (Fig. S13).

The FTIR spectra of NCDs, ZnF, and ZnF@x%NCDs (here, x = 10, 20, and 30) in the range of 4000-400 cm−1 are represented in (Fig. 5 ). The bands between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 identify the N–H stretching vibrations in NCDs, which become more prominent with the increase in NCDs concentration in the composite materials. The spectrum of ZnF consists of five peaks at ∼435, ∼550, ∼1384, ∼1640, and ∼3440 cm−1. The broad peaks at 1640 and 3440 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending and stretching vibrations of adsorbed water [25]. Two bands at 435 and 550 cm−1 appear due to vibrations of metal-oxygen bonds (Fe–O and Zn–O) at octahedral and tetrahedral sites, respectively [9,25]. The small signal at 1384 cm−1 occurs most likely due to C–O stretching from residual oxalate precursors [53,54]. The FTIR spectra of ZnF@x%NCDs show the characteristic peaks of metal-oxygen vibration (in the range of 400–600 cm−1), and a distinct functional group of carbon dots (CC stretching at 1616 cm−1), demonstrating evidence of successful composite formation [55].

The Diffuse Reflectance absorption spectra for ZnF and ZnF@x%NCDs are shown in (Fig. 6 (a)). Direct band gap calculations were performed by obtaining Tauc plots (Fig. 6(b)) using the following equation:
eq. (2)
(
α h ν
)
n
= A
(
h ν −
E
g
)
Here, A = constant, α = absorption coefficient, hν = photon energy, and n = 2 for direct energy transitions.

The as obtained direct bandgap for ZnF is 2.03 eV which is consistent with the literature report [42]. Interestingly, the loading of NCDs on ZnF resulted in a slight reduction of bandgap to ∼1.91 eV. The as-prepared NCDs have a bandgap of 1.73 eV, as obtained from UV–Vis DRS spectra (Fig. S14). The decline in band gap occurs likely due to the formation of intermediate states at the interface of heterojunction of NCDs and ZnF [56].

LSV analysis was done to measure the current, which is directly proportional to the concentration of charge carriers. (Fig. S15) shows the photocurrent density of ZnF and ZnF@20%NCDs electrodes at different voltages in the dark and under light illumination. The current density of both photocatalysts was on the anodic side, thus indicating the n-type nature of both photocatalysts. The current density of ZnF@20%NCDs (0.4 mA/cm2) is higher than the bare ZnF (0.06 mA/cm2). Also, the current density increases under light illumination compared to dark conditions. These findings suggest that the higher photocurrent density of n-type ZnF@20%NCDs semiconductor is due to the generation of photo-induced electrons, which facilitates better photocatalytic activity [22].

Nyquist plots were obtained under light illumination using the EIS technique to probe the interfacial charge transfer resistance. These curves usually consist of two regions, a semi-circle and a straight-line [9]. The smaller the arc radius obtained, the more influential the charge separation process. For the ZnF and NCDs, the composite heterojunction ZnF@20%NCDs shows a smaller semi-circular curve, indicating lower interfacial charge transfer resistance. Thus, the better photocatalytic efficiency of ZnF@20%NCDs composite could be explained due to the better conductivity, i.e., higher charge separation efficiency and less electron-hole recombination. Fig. 7 (a) shows the Nyquist plots for ZnF, NCDs, and ZnF@20%NCDs under light illumination.

Fig. 7(b) shows the transient photocurrent response of ZnF, NCDs, and ZnF@20%NCDs in 0.2 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution under halogen light illumination. It could be seen that ZnF exhibits low transient photocurrent in comparison to the NCDs; furthermore, ZnF@20 % NCDs possess the highest photocurrent response, nearly five times higher than that of ZnF. These results shed light on the fact that NCDs can act as an efficient charge carrier that helps to separate electron-hole pairs produced in ZnF, which results in a higher transient photocurrent response [32]. Hence, it is attributed to the enhanced overall photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalytic decomposition of CIP in aqueous media was performed under halogen lamp illumination from 400 to 800 nm. Among the ZnF@x%NCDs (x = 10, 20, and 30) photocatalysts, ZnF@20%NCDs show the best activity (Fig. 8 (a)). Fig. 8(b) represents the kinetics of degradation for as-prepared photocatalysts. The UV–Vis absorption spectra for CIP with ZnF and ZnF@x% NCDs (x = 10 and 30) under photocatalytic conditions are shown in (Fig. S16). The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared photocatalysts follows the trend (Fig. 8(c)) as follows:
ZnF @ 20 %
NCDs
>
ZnF @ 10 %
NCDs
>
ZnF @ 30 %
NCDs
>
ZnF

The as-prepared photocatalysts follow the pseudo-first-order kinetics. The order of the reaction kinetics was confirmed by fitting with first-order kinetics using the following equation:
eq. (3)
ln
C t
C 0
= − k t
Here, Ct and C0 indicate the final and initial concentrations, respectively. The value of k is measured from the slope of -ln (Ct/C0) vs. the time plot obtained by linear fitting of curves as demonstrated in (Fig. S17). The value of k, first order rate constant (min−1) obtained for all the photocatalysts is represented in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing photolysis, photocatalysis, and adsorption processes
ce:section.ce:para: To better gain insight into the effect of adsorption and light illumination, a comparative study involving photolysis, adsorption, and photocatalysis was performed using ZnF@20%NCDs, as shown in (Fig. 8(d)). We observed a ∼17 % decline during the photolysis process. Additionally, to understand the effect of adsorption on the CIP degradation, a 0.6 mg/mL photocatalyst was taken in CIP, keeping the initial concentration of CIP the same as before, resulting in a 40 % decline in CIP concentration. Thus, it could be inferred that adsorption plays a vital role in CIP depletion. However, in the photocatalysis process, CIP concentration decreased to 79.2 % under similar conditions, nearly four times the depletion observed in the photolysis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different parameters on photocatalytic studies

ce:section.ce:para: The initial pH of the solution plays a crucial role in the photocatalytic reaction. The modification in the pH alters the interaction between the photocatalyst and CIP molecules. In our study, photocatalytic performance increases from ∼64 % to ∼79%as the pH increases from 4 to 7- as shown in (Fig. 9 (a)). However, the efficiency decreases at pH 9 (∼78 %) and pH 11 (∼72 %). Ciprofloxacin exists in an uncharged, zwitterionic form in the pH range of 6.0–7.0 [57]. The cationic and anionic forms of CIP dominate at pH < 5.0 and pH > 8.0, respectively. On the other hand, zeta potential analysis of photocatalysts indicates the existence of positive surface charges at pH 4 and 7, while negative charge at pH 9 and 11, as shown in Table S3. Under acidic conditions (pH 4), both ZnF@20%NCDs and CIP are positively charged, thus resulting in repulsive interaction and less photocatalytic efficiency. While at pH = 7, favorable interactions between zwitterionic CIP and positively charged photocatalysts result in an upsurge in the photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, under alkaline conditions at pH 9 and 11, a marginal decline in the overall photocatalytic efficiency was observed due to the unfavorable interactions between negatively charged photocatalysts and anionic CIP.

Different dosages of ZnF@20 % NCDs (5, 10, 15, 30, and 45 mg) were dispersed in 25 mL of aqueous CIP solution (0.01 mg/mL) to optimize the photocatalyst concentration. As the photocatalyst amount increased, the degradation percentage initially increased and then started declining–(Fig. 9(b)). Thus, as the amount of catalyst was increased, the overall photodegradation efficiency slightly decreased. The maximum degradation was observed with 0.6 mg/mL photocatalyst dispersion. In contrast, at a loading of more than 0.6 mg/mL, there is a marginal decline in efficiency due to the aggregation of photocatalysts or more scattering from suspended photocatalyst nanoparticles [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial concentration of CIP
ce:section.ce:para: The initial concentration of CIP was varied from 10 to 40 mg/L to probe the role of CIP concentration on the degradation efficiency. We observed that the photocatalytic degradation efficiency decreased from 79.2 % to 48.5 % as the concentration of CIP increased from 10 to 40 mg/L– (Fig. 9(c)). This might be due to the saturation of the active surface in the presence of a higher amount of CIP.

Recyclability experiments were done for three consecutive runs to demonstrate the robustness of the photocatalyst, as represented in (Fig. S18). The photocatalytic performance remains similar for the first and second cycles; however, a ∼3 % decline was observed in the third cycle. The recycled ZnF@20 % NCDs after the third cycle was further characterized by XPS. As shown in (Fig. S19), there are no significant changes in the XPS spectra of recycled photocatalysts compared to unused photocatalysts, as represented in (Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of other fluoroquinolone antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: Apart from CIP, norfloxacin (NOR) degradation was performed with ZnF@20 % NCDs.We have added 0.02 mg/mL of NOR solution with 0.6 mg/mL of photocatalyst. The dark equilibrium was maintained for 30 min, followed by light illumination for 240 min. We observed a 77.8 % decline in NOR concentration, as presented in (Fig. S20). From these results, it could be suggested that the as-prepared photocatalyst could also be used to degrade other fluoroquinolone drugs.

After the photodegradation of ciprofloxacin using ZnF@20%NCDs at pH = 7, several oxidation products were identified by LC-MS technique. The mass spectrum of pristine CIP and transformed products are shown in (Fig. S21). The chemical structures and mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) of the oxidation products are shown in Table S4. Most of the transformed products (m/z- 362, 334, 306, 291, 263) are produced by transforming the piperazine ring. Our results suggest that the degradation is likely to proceed through the oxidation of piperazine to dialdehyde derivative (m/z = 362), which further transformed into monoaldehyde derivative (m/z = 334), as proposed by Caruso and coworkers. The monoaldehyde derivative further undergoes stepwise cleavage to defluorinated product (m/z = 245) [59,60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of CIP using ZnF@20%NCDs

ce:section.ce:para: Trapping experiments were performed to identify the active species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of CIP. In this reactive oxygen species scavenging experiment, p-BQ (para-Benzoquinone), EDTA (ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were employed to scavenge the superoxide radicals (O.- 2), holes (h+), and hydroxyl radicals (. OH) respectively. Fig. 10 (a) demonstrates the role of reactive species that play a crucial role in the photodegradation of CIP. The decrease in the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of CIP in the presence of holes scavenging agent EDTA suggests a hole-based mechanism towards the photoactivity of composite material. Our finding suggests that the oxidation of CIP proceeds through the formation of CIP radical cation by direct hole transfer from the valence band of the photocatalyst to adsorbed CIP [14].

Next, Mott-Schottky measurements were done to determine the type of semiconductor and its corresponding flat band potential value (Efb). NCDs and ZnF (Figs. S22(a and b)) have a positive slope; thus, both act as n-type semiconducting materials. Further, extending the slope towards the x-axis gives us the flat band potential value (Efb) vs. Ag/AgCl. Also, the positive slope obtained in the ZnF@20 % NCDs confirms the formation of n-n type semiconducting heterojunction (Fig. S23(c)). These results also complemented the results obtained from LSV measurements. The flat band potential (Efb) of ZnF, NCDs, and ZnF@20%NCDs are calculated as −0.79, −0.62, and −0.95 V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively. For n-type semiconductors, the flat band potential (Efb) can be approximated as conduction band potential [59]. Furthermore, the experimentally obtained flat band potential values (Efb) vs. Ag/AgCl can be converted into the Normal Hydrogen Electrode (NHE) using the following equation:
eq. (4)

Thus, the conduction band potentials (ECB) of ZnF, NCDs, and ZnF@20%NCDs at vs. NHE were measured as −0.59, −0.42, and −0.75 eV, respectively. In addition, the valence band positions (EVB) can be obtained using the following equation [45,59]:
eq. (5)

E g

The energy band gaps (Eg) of ZnF, NCDs, and ZnF@20%NCDs are obtained (from DRS spectra) as 2.03, 1.73, and 1.91 eV, respectively. Using these Eg values in the above equation, the positions of the valence band are obtained as 1.44, 1.31, and 1.16 eV for ZnF, NCDs, and ZnF@20%NCDs vs. NHE, respectively. Based on the values obtained, the possible photocatalytic mechanism for as-prepared n-n heterojunction can be proposed, as demonstrated in (Fig. 10(b)).

Charge carriers (e− and h+) are separated when a photocatalyst is exposed to light. Since NCDs act as efficient electron acceptors, the electrons from the conduction band of ZnF are transferred to the NCDs under illumination. Considering the valence band of both ZnF and NCDs is less positive than the redox potential of (OH−/.OH (+2.34 eV)), the hydroxyl radical generation is not thermodynamically feasible. However, the holes in the valence band of ZnF could directly interact with the adsorbed CIP, as corroborated by scavenger experiments. Hole transfer-based photocatalytic degradation using zinc ferrites has been reported earlier [14]. Interfacial electron transfer from ZnF to NCDs facilitates electron-hole separation and consequent hole transfer from ZnF to CIP. Thus, the overall photodegradation process can be suggested as follows:

In summary, we used facile microwave synthesis to prepare ZnF@x%NCDs composites. This study sheds light on the synthesis and characteristics of zinc ferrite/NCDs composites that remove antibiotics like ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin in the aqueous medium. ZnF@x%NCDs displayed better photocatalytic efficiency in comparison to bare ZnF under visible light illumination due to the following reasons, facile method of synthesis, larger surface area, narrower band gap, along with better charge carrier ability, which is facilitated by the presence of NCDs. Carbon dots acted as a prominent component for the efficient transfer of charge carriers during photocatalysis. The narrow bandgap, efficient charge separation, and characterization results favor the highest photocatalytic efficiency of ZnF@20%NCDs among the prepared composites. The optimized conditions for the maximum depletion of CIP using ZnF@20%NCDs were neutral pH conditions, with an initial CIP concentration of 0.01 mg/mL and an illumination time of 180 min. The recyclability of ZnF@20%NCDs as photocatalytic material was evaluated for three cycles without a significant loss of photocatalytic efficiency. We have also observed a 77.2 % decline in norfloxacin (0.02 mg/mL) after 240 min of illumination. Thus, it could be concluded that the composition of ZnF with NCDs could be a prominent strategy to enhance the photocatalytic activity for environmental remediation.
